does: This paper introduces an adversarial parametric editing framework that directly modifies Vision-Language Models to reduce hallucinations where outputs diverge from visual content. The method operates through three stages: first, constructing an activation dataset with grounded positive examples and hallucinatory negative examples; second, identifying critical parameter clusters within the model that are prone to hallucination by analyzing differential hidden states between these examples; third, adversarially editing these parameters to better align visual feature integration with linguistic outputs. The framework directly optimizes model parameters rather than relying on decoding-time calibration, enabling end-to-end trainable mitigation that addresses the root causes of over-reliance on linguistic priors and insufficient visual grounding.
connects: This work builds on recent recognition that VLMs suffer from hallucinations due to imbalanced visual-linguistic integration, extending prior heuristic decoding strategies like prompt calibration and output filtering that operate at inference time without modifying the model itself. It responds to the limitation that non-trainable approaches cannot optimize hallucination mitigation, drawing on broader literature examining how language models encode and prioritize different information sources. The paper also connects to parameter editing and model surgery techniques developed for knowledge updates and behavioral modification in large language models, adapting these methods to the multimodal setting where hallucinations arise from cross-modal alignment failures.
enables: This framework opens pathways for more direct intervention in multimodal model behavior by establishing systematic methods to identify and modify hallucination-prone components. It enables future work on understanding which specific parameters encode linguistic bias versus visual grounding in VLMs, potentially allowing fine-grained control over model behaviors. The approach could extend to other forms of output misalignment in vision-language tasks and facilitate development of efficient personalized editing strategies without full model retraining. It also points toward adversarial training paradigms that explicitly balance multiple modalities, potentially informing safer and more controllable deployment of VLMs in high-stakes applications where hallucination-free outputs are critical.
